questions.question: Explain the difference between admissible and consistent heuristics with examples.
keyPoints: Admissible heuristic never overestimates: h(n) ≤ h*(n)
Consistent (monotonic) satisfies triangle inequality: h(n) ≤ c + h(n')
Manhattan distance is both admissible and consistent for 8-puzzle
Euclidean distance is admissible but not necessarily consistent for all graphs
Every consistent heuristic is admissible but converse not true

questions.question: What is heuristic dominance? If heuristic h1 dominates h2, explain why A* using h1 expands fewer nodes.
keyPoints: h1 dominates h2 if h1(n) ≥ h2(n) for all states
Higher heuristic values make f(n) = g(n) + h(n) larger
A* expands nodes in order of increasing f(n)
With dominant heuristic, goal has lower f-value earlier
Fewer nodes need expansion before reaching goal threshold

questions.question: Describe how to derive a heuristic from a relaxed problem with an example.
keyPoints: Relaxed problem removes constraints from original problem
Solution of relaxed problem provides admissible heuristic
8-puzzle: relaxed allows tiles to move anywhere (Manhattan distance)
15-puzzle: pattern database uses subset of tiles
The optimal cost of relaxed problem ≤ optimal cost of original

questions.question: Compare Greedy Best-First Search and A* Search algorithms.
keyPoints: Greedy uses only h(n), A* uses g(n) + h(n)
Greedy is not guaranteed optimal, A* with admissible h is optimal
Greedy can get stuck in loops, A* is complete
Greedy has better time efficiency but may not find optimal
A* balances optimality and efficiency with good heuristic

questions.question: Explain IDA* and its advantages over A*.
keyPoints: IDA* uses iterative deepening with f-cost threshold
Starts with h(start) as threshold, increases to next highest f-value
Uses exponential less memory than A* (stores only current path)
Suitable for large state spaces where A* would run out of memory
Maintains optimality with admissible heuristic

questions.question: What is the optimality property of A*? Explain why no other algorithm can be more efficient with an admissible heuristic.
keyPoints: A* with admissible h(n) is optimally efficient
Any algorithm expanding fewer nodes may miss the optimal solution
A* expands all nodes with f(n) < f*(goal) where f* is optimal cost
This is the minimal superset guarantee for optimal solutions
No algorithm can guarantee optimality while expanding fewer nodes

questions.question: Explain SMA* algorithm and how it handles memory limitations.
keyPoints: SMA* uses all available memory to store search frontier
When memory is full, drops node with highest f-value
Remembers the f-value of dropped node in parent
When all children are dropped, algorithm backtracks
Guarantees completeness if solution fits in memory, and optimality if path fits

questions.question: Explain the A* search algorithm in detail. Discuss its completeness, optimality, time and space complexity. What happens when h(n) = 0?
keyPoints: A* uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n) evaluation function
Completeness: A* is complete if branching factor is finite and all costs are positive
Optimality: A* finds optimal solution if h(n) is admissible
Optimality: A* finds optimal solution if h(n) is consistent (graph search)
Time complexity: O(b^d) exponential in worst case
Space complexity: O(b^d) exponential - stores all generated nodes
If h(n)=0: A* degenerates to Uniform-Cost Search
If h(n)=0: loses heuristic advantage, explores by g(n) only
With perfect heuristic h(n)=h*(n): only optimal path nodes expanded
Trade-off: better h(n) → fewer nodes but more computation per node

questions.question: Describe various methods for designing heuristic functions. Explain with examples how each method works.
keyPoints: Relaxed Problems: Remove constraints, e.g., 8-puzzle Manhattan distance
Pattern Databases: Precompute exact costs for subproblems
Landmarks: Identify must-visit states, use distances from/to landmarks
Statistical Heuristics: Learn from previous problem instances
Example - 8-puzzle: Manhattan distance from tile to goal position
Example - Traveling Salesman: minimum spanning tree heuristic
Example - Rubik's cube: pattern databases for cube subsets
Dominance: Combine multiple heuristics by taking maximum
Composite heuristics provide better (dominant) estimates

questions.question: Discuss memory-bounded heuristic search algorithms. Compare IDA*, RBFS, and SMA* with respect to their memory usage and completeness.
keyPoints: Standard A* has exponential memory requirements
IDA*: Iterative deepening with f-cost threshold, stores only current path
IDA*: Memory O(bd) but may redo much work - exponential time overhead
RBFS: Recursive algorithm, stores current path plus one sibling per level
RBFS: Memory O(bd), can exceed memory if threshold changes dramatically
SMA*: Uses all available memory, drops worst f-value nodes when full
SMA*: Remembers dropped node's f-value in parent for future consideration
All three guarantee optimality with admissible heuristics
All three are complete for finite state spaces
Trade-offs: IDA* time vs memory, RBFS simplicity, SMA* best memory utilization

questions.question: Consider the 8-puzzle problem. (a) Define the state space. (b) What is the branching factor? (c) Compute the number of reachable states. (d) Explain how Manhattan distance is derived as a heuristic. (e) If we use h(n) = number of misplaced tiles, is this admissible?
keyPoints: (a) States: 9! configurations, but only half reachable (parity constraint)
(b) Branching factor ~3 (average 2-4 moves per state)
(c) 9!/2 = 181,440 reachable states
(d) Relaxed problem: tiles can move directly to goal position ignoring obstacles; sum of distances = Manhattan distance
(e) Yes, misplaced tiles ≤ Manhattan distance, never overestimates
Both heuristics are admissible, Manhattan dominates misplaced tiles
With Manhattan, A* for 8-puzzle expands ~1000 nodes vs millions for BFS
